scenarioName: WPW with Atrial Fibrillation — Irregularly Irregular Wide Complex Tachycardia

scenarioStory.history: 34-year-old previously healthy male was on the treadmill at his gym when he developed sudden onset of palpitations, chest fluttering, and a sensation of impending doom. He stopped, sat down, and felt lightheaded but did not lose consciousness. A bystander called 911. Patient reports two prior 'episodes' over the past two years where his heart 'raced' for about 20 minutes and self-resolved; he never sought care because each one stopped on its own. He has been told once that he has 'a short PR or something' on a sports physical EKG but doesn't remember the details. Denies fever, recent illness, drug use, caffeine excess, weight loss, or hot/cold intolerance. Drinks alcohol socially. No prescription medications. He is now anxious, alert, mildly dyspneic, with a borderline blood pressure.
scenarioStory.course: Initial Contact: EMS arrives to find a young, athletic, but visibly distressed male seated on a gym bench. He is alert, pale, mildly diaphoretic, and tachypneic. Radial pulse is rapid and irregular and difficult to count manually. The teaching point begins now: a previously healthy young patient with an irregularly irregular wide complex tachycardia must trigger immediate consideration of pre-excited atrial fibrillation (WPW with AFib) and the rule against AV nodal blockers. → Procainamide for stable pre-excited AFib: Crew correctly identifies pre-excited AFib (WPW with AFib) and avoids AV nodal blockers. Patient is hemodynamically borderline-stable (SBP 92, mentation intact, no ischemic chest pain) — pharmacologic conversion with procainamide is the protocol-preferred approach. Procainamide infusion at 20–50 mg/min is initiated with continuous monitoring of QRS width, BP, and rhythm. Endpoints are: arrhythmia suppression, hypotension, QRS widening > 50%, or max dose 17 mg/kg. → Converted to sinus; WPW visible at baseline: After approximately 600–800 mg of procainamide infused at 30 mg/min, the rhythm converts to sinus with a short PR interval and a small but unmistakable delta wave — the classic WPW baseline. HR settles in the 80s with normal blood pressure. Crew transitions to maintenance infusion 1–4 mg/min, transports for cardiology evaluation and likely electrophysiology referral, and provides bedside handoff with the conversion strip.

patientInformation.patientCondition: Sudden onset of palpitations, lightheadedness, chest fluttering, and shortness of breath that began ~20 minutes ago at the gym. Patient describes 'my heart is beating so fast it feels like it's going to come out of my chest.'

scenarioEvents.name: Initial Contact
scenarioEvents.description: EMS arrives to find a young, athletic, but visibly distressed male seated on a gym bench. He is alert, pale, mildly diaphoretic, and tachypneic. Radial pulse is rapid and irregular and difficult to count manually. The teaching point begins now: a previously healthy young patient with an irregularly irregular wide complex tachycardia must trigger immediate consideration of pre-excited atrial fibrillation (WPW with AFib) and the rule against AV nodal blockers.
relatedChecklist: Primary survey; manage airway; titrate O2 to 94–98% saturation
Cardiac monitor; defib pads on; 12-lead EKG immediately
Establish IV access (large-bore proximal site)
Check blood glucose — rule out hypoglycemia as a tachycardia driver
Classify rhythm: irregularly irregular + wide QRS = pre-excited AFib (WPW) until proven otherwise
Determine stability: AMS / chest pain / SOB / hypotension / shock?

scenarioEvents.name: Procainamide for stable pre-excited AFib
scenarioEvents.description: Crew correctly identifies pre-excited AFib (WPW with AFib) and avoids AV nodal blockers. Patient is hemodynamically borderline-stable (SBP 92, mentation intact, no ischemic chest pain) — pharmacologic conversion with procainamide is the protocol-preferred approach. Procainamide infusion at 20–50 mg/min is initiated with continuous monitoring of QRS width, BP, and rhythm. Endpoints are: arrhythmia suppression, hypotension, QRS widening > 50%, or max dose 17 mg/kg.
relatedChecklist: Withhold adenosine, diltiazem, metoprolol, digoxin (pre-excited AFib)
Procainamide IV at 20–50 mg/min (max 17 mg/kg = ~1394 mg for 82 kg); stop at suppression / hypotension / QRS widens > 50%
If procainamide unsuccessful, amiodarone 150 mg IV over 10 min (NOT before procainamide in WPW)
Sync mode pre-armed; energy 120–150 J biphasic ready if patient deteriorates
Use only one antidysrhythmic at a time; document each push

scenarioEvents.name: Converted to sinus; WPW visible at baseline
scenarioEvents.description: After approximately 600–800 mg of procainamide infused at 30 mg/min, the rhythm converts to sinus with a short PR interval and a small but unmistakable delta wave — the classic WPW baseline. HR settles in the 80s with normal blood pressure. Crew transitions to maintenance infusion 1–4 mg/min, transports for cardiology evaluation and likely electrophysiology referral, and provides bedside handoff with the conversion strip.
relatedChecklist: Procainamide maintenance 1–4 mg/min IV; reassess BP and QRS each interval
Save initial, mid-treatment, and post-conversion strips; document times and totals
Transport to ED with cardiology / EP capability; bedside handoff with strips

scenarioEvents.name: Adenosine given → VFib arrest
scenarioEvents.description: Crew misread the irregular wide complex tachycardia as a regular SVT and gave adenosine 6 mg IV. Within seconds the AV node was blocked and conduction was forced exclusively down the accessory pathway, the ventricular rate accelerated to >300, and the rhythm degenerated into ventricular fibrillation. Patient is now pulseless. Immediate high-quality CPR and defibrillation are required.
relatedChecklist: Confirm pulselessness; immediate high-quality CPR
Defibrillate 200 J biphasic (manufacturer recommendation); resume CPR immediately
Recognize iatrogenic VFib; document AV nodal blocker as cause

scenarioEvents.name: Unstable → synchronized cardioversion
scenarioEvents.description: Patient deteriorates with worsening hypotension (SBP < 80), AMS, and chest pain before pharmacology can take effect. The pre-excited AFib is now an unstable irregular wide complex tachycardia. Crew correctly transitions to synchronized cardioversion at biphasic 120–150 J with sedation as feasible. This is a valid alternative path that does not punish the crew for selecting cardioversion when the clinical condition warrants it.
relatedChecklist: Synchronized cardioversion 120–150 J biphasic (manufacturer recommendation)
If responsive: pre-shock sedation/analgesia (e.g., midazolam, fentanyl) per pain mgmt
Reassess rhythm and pulse after each shock; escalate energy if not converted

Sync mode pre-armed; energy 120–150 J biphasic ready if patient deteriorates

Procainamide maintenance 1–4 mg/min IV; reassess BP and QRS each interval
Save initial, mid-treatment, and post-conversion strips; document times and totals
Transport to ED with cardiology / EP capability; bedside handoff with strips

Synchronized cardioversion 120–150 J biphasic (manufacturer recommendation)
If responsive: pre-shock sedation/analgesia (e.g., midazolam, fentanyl) per pain mgmt
Reassess rhythm and pulse after each shock; escalate energy if not converted
